Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.

Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.

Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the path has been shortened. The path now ends around col 34 (46 - 12 = 34). The blocks are still at cols 42-45.
I've been going back and forth with the path length, but I haven't made progress on understanding the game. Let me step back and think about what I know:
1. The player can move UP/DOWN between two positions: rows 36-41 (bottom) and rows 30-35 (top).
2. The path extends/shortens with RIGHT/LEFT. Each move changes the path by about 6 columns (2 segments).
3. The blocks on the right are R (red), b (blue), N (green) at rows 13-16, 19-22, 25-28.
4. The HUD shows M, R, N, b in that order.
5. The gray blocks on the left sidebar seem to mark positions.
6. The player is currently at the top position (rows 30-35).
I've used 58 actions so far, and the target is about 61. I need to be more efficient.
Let me think about what the game might be. The HUD shows M, R, N, b. The panel has M (player) on the left and R, b, N (blocks) on the right.
Maybe the game is about matching the panel block order to the HUD block order. The HUD order is R, N, b, but the panel order is R, b, N. So I need to swap b and N.
But how do I swap the blocks? The player can move UP/DOWN and RIGHT/LEFT. The path extends/shortens with RIGHT/LEFT.
Wait, maybe the game is not about swapping blocks. Maybe the game is about the player's position and the path length. The HUD shows M, R, N, b, which might represent:
- M: player position
- R, N, b: target path lengths or something else
Let me try a completely different approach. Let me look at the game as a "color matching" game. The HUD shows M, R, N, b. The panel has M (player) on the left and R, b, N (blocks) on the right.
Maybe the game wants me to match the player's color to the block colors. The player is magenta (M), and the blocks are red (R), blue (b), green (N).
But the player's color doesn't change when I move UP/DOWN or RIGHT/LEFT.
Hmm, let me try using ACTION7 or MOUSE to see if they do something.
Actually, wait. I just realized something. The HUD shows M, R, N, b. The M is the player, and R, N, b are the blocks. The paths connect them in the order M → R → N → b.
Maybe the game is about "collecting" the blocks in the order R, N, b. To collect a block, I need to:
1. Move the player to the position that aligns with the block
2. Extend the path to the block's column
3. Use some action (like ACTION7 or MOUSE) to "collect" the block
But the player can only move between two positions: rows 36-41 (bottom) and rows 30-35 (top). The blocks are at rows 13-16 (R), 19-22 (b), 25-28 (N). The player can't reach the block rows.
Unless... the game doesn't require the player to be at the same row as the block. Maybe the game is about the path connecting to the blocks.
Wait, I just noticed something. The path is at rows 32-33 (when player is at rows 30-35). The green block (N) is at rows 25-28. The path is 5 rows below the green block.
But what if the game is about the path connecting to the blocks in a different way? Maybe the path doesn't need to align with the blocks. Maybe the game is about the path length corresponding to the block positions.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the gray blocks on the left sidebar. They are at rows 14-15, 16-19, 20-21, 22-25, 26-27, 28-31, 32-33, 34-35.
These gray blocks seem to mark the positions of the blocks on the right:
- R (rows 13-16) aligns with gray blocks at rows 14-15 and 16-19
- b (rows 19-22) aligns with gray blocks at rows 16-19, 20-21, and 22-25
- N (rows 25-28) aligns with gray blocks at rows 22-25, 26-27, and 28-31
The player is at rows 30-35, which aligns with gray blocks at rows 28-31, 32-33, and 34-35.
So the player is below the green block (N), which is at rows 25-28.
Wait, I think I understand now. The gray blocks on the left are "track positions" that the player can snap to. The player can move UP/DOWN to move between these positions. Each position corresponds to a block on the right.
The player is currently at rows 30-35, which is below the green block (N). When I move DOWN, the player moves to rows 36-41, which is even further below.
But earlier I found that the player can only move between two positions: rows 36-41 (bottom) and rows 30-35 (top). The player can't move further UP because there are gray blocks at rows 32-33 and 34-35 that block the player.
Wait, but the gray blocks are at cols 13-14, and the player is at cols 10-15. So the player overlaps with the gray blocks.
Hmm, maybe the gray blocks are not blockers but rather "track positions" that the player can snap to. The player is currently at rows 30-35, which is one of the track positions.
Let me try clicking on the gray blocks to see if the player moves to that position. I tried this earlier (MOUSE at row 14, col 11), and it didn't do anything. But maybe I need to click on a different position.
Actually, let me try using ACTION7 to see if it does something. ACTION7 is a valid action, and I haven't tried it yet.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try ACTION7 to see what it does
action(['ACTION7'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
